  # Resources and Media
  
  ### [**8ightWays® to Prevent Cancer**](https://siteman.wustl.edu/wp-admin/post.php?post=1941&action=edit&classic-editor)
  
  The power of healthy living is hard to overestimate. A large percentage of cancers and many other chronic diseases can be prevented by things like eating a healthy diet, getting regular exercise, keeping weight in check, and getting important screening tests. The best thing? These are things under your control. Take some simple steps to improve your health, and help your family do the same.
  
  ## 8ightWays® to Stay Healthy and Prevent Cancer

  ## Your Disease Risk®
  
  Find out your risk of 12 cancers and 6 other important chronic diseases – and get personalized tips for preventing them.
  
  [Take the assessment](https://siteman.wustl.edu/prevention/ydr/)
  
  ## In Good Taste
  
  Division of Public Health Sciences faculty members are featured monthly in Feast Magazine and the St. Louis Post-Dispatch explaining health and prevention benefits of specific foods.
  
  [Read here](https://www.feastmagazine.com/sponsored/in-good-taste/)
  
  ## For Your Health
  
  Division of Public Health Sciences chief Dr. Graham Colditz writes a monthly column, syndicated in small town newspapers across the country, on various health topics.
  
  [Read here](https://siteman.wustl.edu/category/for-your-health/)
  
  ### **Resource Guides**
  
  Our resource guides are comprehensive directories to the resources available in the St. Louis region for accessing cancer screening and treatment, and the services available before, during, and after treatment. You can download your guide by clicking on the relevant cover below. Printed copies may be available for community partners–for more information, email [[email protected]](/cdn-cgi/l/email-protection) .
  
  ## Lung Cancer Resource Guide
  
  [Download PDF](https://siteman.wustl.edu/wp-content/uploads/2023/01/Final-SCC-PECaD-lung-resource-guide_HIRES.pdf)
  
  ## Cancer News in Context
  
  Tackling popular news stories related to cancer prevention and screening, Cancer News in Context tames the hype with a big-picture outlook and important take home messages. This link will take you to the blog, which is hosted by WashU Medicine.
  
  [Read the blog](https://publichealthsciences.wustl.edu/community-focus/cancer-news-in-context/)
  
  ### Clinical Trials Brochures
  
  We’ve developed two new brochures to help inform people on how they can participate in clinical trials, and why diversity in participants of clinical trials is important.
  
  ## Clinical Trials
  
  [Download PDF](https://siteman.wustl.edu/wp-content/uploads/2023/01/557196-SCC-Clinical-Trials-flyer-Patient_FINAL.pdf)
  
  ## Diversity in Clinical Trials
  
  [Download PDF](https://siteman.wustl.edu/wp-content/uploads/2023/01/SCC-Clinical-Trials-flyer-Consumer_FINAL.pdf)
  
  ## Data Resources
  
  We offer a number of resources for key data about public health, including community snapshots about **[housing](https://siteman.wustl.edu/wp-content/uploads/2023/03/Community-Snapshot_Housing-data-brief_07232022_LaVidaCo.pdf)** and **[transportation](https://siteman.wustl.edu/wp-content/uploads/2023/03/Community-Snapshot_2021-02-12-Transportation-data-brief.pdf)** as well as **[MCR county-specific data](https://medicine.missouri.edu/centers-institutes-labs/cancer-registry-research-center/data/county)** . You can also submit a **[data request redcap form](https://redcap.wustl.edu/redcap/surveys/?s=NA8RLKH3DM)** .
  
  [Explore Siteman’s SCC dashboards](https://sites.wustl.edu/sccdashboards/) [Explore the Missouri Cancer Registry and Research Center](https://medicine.missouri.edu/centers-institutes-labs/cancer-registry-research-center)
  
  ### **Learn about preventing skin cancer**
  
  The Division of Public Health Sciences worked with the Department of Dermatology at WashU Medicine, Siteman Cancer Center, the American Cancer Society, Missouri Dermatological Society, Blackout Melanoma, Miles Against Melanoma, Warriors Against Melanoma, and Our Mark on Melanoma to create public services announcements aimed at educating the public on the dangers of indoor tanning.

  [The Benefits of Smoking Cessation and Prevention](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6_hTJt2Qhn8)
  
  [How to Prevent Skin Cancer](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IphuX0fPwcM)

  [Watch here](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Uiy5TTqSns4&list=PLAvCzAIAfHmHJEPOMW7ymhV9mYWthruHn)
  
  ### Audio/Podcasts
  
  Many of our affiliated researchers share their expertise on podcasts and other audio formats. Here you’ll find links to recent podcast episodes on various topics that you can share with your communities.
  
    + Health Horizons with Keisha Windham and Kim Thorwegen – The St. Louis Podcast: Episode #83 [Listen now.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=prtISshWA34)
    + [A “PSA” on PSA Tests for Prostate Cancer: Saving Men’s Lives](https://siteman.wustl.edu/podcast/a-psa-on-psa-tests-for-prostate-cancer-saving-men-s-lives/)
    + April 2022: Dr. Graham Colditz was featured on the Master Minds podcast, where he was interviewed on the topic of **“Preventing Cancer and Chronic Disease.”** [Listen here](https://podcasts.apple.com/us/podcast/preventing-cancer-and-chronic-disease-with-dr/id1533106653?i=1000555914535) .
    + March 2022: Dr. Erin Linnenbringer was interviewed on the GENEish podcast, talking about the **role of a genetic counselor, her love for research, as well as how psychological and genetic health affect us** . [Listen here.](https://open.spotify.com/episode/1pjqKiWhrBpsLtS80K5Aw2?si=t6v4WIX9R2WhAmbyv7BP7A)
    + March 2022: Dr. Liz Salerno appeared on the PQI podcast, discussing **“Physical Activity, Cognition, and the Cancer Patient.”** [Listen here.](https://the-pqi-podcast.simplecast.com/episodes/s2-ep8-physical-activity-cognition-and-the-cancer-patient-elizabeth-salerno-phd-mph)
    + February 2022: Dr. Graham Colditz was interviewed for the Breast Cancer Research Foundation podcast on the topic of **how diet and lifestyle influence breast cancer risk** . [Listen here](https://www.bcrf.org/blog/bcrf-2022-podcast-graham-colditz-breast-cancer/) .
